metadata.career_relevance: Flipkart's product detail page fetches data from 12 different microservices (product, inventory, reviews, seller, shipping, offers, images, recommendations...). With REST: 12 separate API calls from the frontend. With GraphQL: one query that specifies exactly the fields needed — product name, first 3 reviews, current price, estimated delivery. GraphQL is used at Flipkart, Razorpay (merchant dashboard), Swiggy (menu API), and Zerodha (portfolio queries). After this topic, you can design GraphQL schemas for real APIs.

hook.what_you_will_build: Flipkart product catalog GraphQL schema with queries and mutations.

concept_explanation.graphql_vs_rest:
**GraphQL vs REST:**

```
REST:
  GET /products/PROD-123          → entire product (all fields)
  GET /products/PROD-123/reviews  → all reviews
  Multiple endpoints, multiple calls
  Over-fetching: backend sends fields frontend doesn't need
  Under-fetching: one call doesn't have enough (need a second)
  Versioning: /v1/products, /v2/products — version proliferation

GraphQL:
  POST /graphql                  → one endpoint for everything
  Client specifies EXACTLY the fields it needs
  Zero over-fetching: only requested fields sent
  Zero under-fetching: one query can nest multiple resources
  No versioning: add new fields (backwards compatible), deprecate old ones

USE GRAPHQL WHEN:
  - Multiple clients (web, iOS, Android) need different fields
  - Data is highly interconnected (product → reviews → seller → rating)
  - Over-fetching is hurting mobile performance

USE REST WHEN:
  - Simple CRUD without complex relationships
  - File upload/download (GraphQL handles poorly)
  - Webhooks and event notifications (REST + HTTP well-suited)
  - Team is unfamiliar with GraphQL (learning curve real)
```

visual_aid.description: GraphQL schema diagram. Center: single /graphql endpoint. Left side: three clients (Web, iOS Android, Admin dashboard) each sending different query shapes to the same endpoint. Right side: GraphQL resolvers connecting to different data sources (Product DB, Reviews Service, Seller DB, Shipping API). Schema box in the middle shows: type Query { product, products }, type Mutation { createProduct, updatePrice }. Annotation: 'One schema, many clients, each gets exactly the fields they need.'
visual_aid.alt_text: GraphQL: one endpoint, multiple clients each requesting different field subsets

worked_example.scenario: Design Flipkart product catalog GraphQL schema.
worked_example.problem_statement:
1. Product type with id, name, price (nested), category (enum), reviews list
2. Review type: id, rating, comment, author
3. Query type: product(id), products(category), searchProducts(keyword)
4. Mutation type: createProduct(input), updatePrice
5. Input type: CreateProductInput
6. Validate schema is correct GraphQL SDL

step_by_step_solution.details: Use ! (non-null) for: ID fields (always has an ID if it exists), name/required fields. Omit ! for: optional fields (description, discount). List: [Product!]! means the list is always returned (never null) and each item in the list is non-null. [Product] means the list itself could be null AND items could be null. For queries returning single items: product(id): Product (nullable) is better than product(id): Product! because if the product doesn't exist, returning null is cleaner than an error.

worked_example.expected_output:
# Valid GraphQL SDL — passes schema validation
# query: { product(id: "PROD-1") { name price { amount currency } } }
# → fetches ONLY name and price.amount and price.currency
# → seller, reviews, images NOT fetched (not requested)
things_to_notice: ID! scalar: GraphQL's built-in opaque identifier type. Clients treat it as a string but shouldn't parse it. Always non-null for an entity's own ID.
Input types vs object types: CreateProductInput is an INPUT type — only for arguments. Product is an OBJECT type — only for return values. You can't use Product as a mutation argument directly.
SDL is the contract: the schema is the contract between frontend and backend. Once published, adding fields is backwards-compatible. Removing fields is breaking — deprecate first with @deprecated.
Queries are GET-equivalent (safe, idempotent); Mutations are POST/PUT/DELETE-equivalent (may have side effects). This distinction matters for caching.

industry_examples.use_case: Merchant dashboard shows: payment volume, settlement status, dispute count, recent transactions. Each widget needs different data. REST: 4 API calls per page. GraphQL: one query per page load with exactly the fields each widget needs. Mobile app: smaller query (fewer fields). Web admin: larger query (all details). Same schema, different queries per client.

applications.interview_relevance: 'Why use GraphQL instead of REST for our product?' → Client-specified fields: each client gets exactly what it needs (mobile: lightweight, admin: full details). One round trip: nested data in one query (product + reviews + seller). No versioning: add fields backwards-compatibly. Trade-offs: more complex server-side (resolvers, N+1), harder to cache (all POST to /graphql), learning curve. Best for: multiple clients needing different data shapes from interconnected data.
applications.real_world_workflow: GraphQL adoption: (1) design schema collaboratively (frontend + backend), (2) implement backend resolvers one type at a time, (3) use GraphQL Playground for exploration and testing, (4) add to existing REST service incrementally (both /api/rest and /graphql can coexist), (5) monitor: track slow queries by field, add DataLoader for N+1 (T3).

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Using Product type directly as a mutation argument: `createProduct(product: Product!)`. GraphQL doesn't allow object types as inputs — only scalar types and input types.
common_gaps.remediation: Always create a separate input type: `input CreateProductInput { name: String! price: Float! }`. Input types are prefixed with the operation name and suffixed with 'Input'. They can only contain scalar types, enum types, and other input types — not object types with resolvers.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Mutation argument uses an object type (type keyword) instead of an input type (input keyword) — GraphQL schema validation error: 'Expected type to be input type'

key_takeaways: GraphQL: one endpoint (/graphql), client specifies exact fields needed
Zero over-fetching: only requested fields sent; zero under-fetching: nested in one query
Schema SDL: type, input, enum, scalar, Query, Mutation, Subscription
Non-null (!): use for IDs and required fields; nullable for optional fields
Input types: for mutation arguments; Object types: for return values (can't mix!)
Single nullable return from query: product(id): Product (not Product!) — null if not found
Backwards compatible: add fields freely; deprecate old ones with @deprecated
GraphQL vs REST: use GraphQL for interconnected data + multiple clients; REST for simple CRUD
Introspection: clients can query the schema itself (__schema, __type)
SDL is the contract: frontend and backend agree on schema before implementation

self_check_questions.question: A Swiggy engineer says: 'I want to add a new `deliveryFee` field to the Order type. Will this break existing clients?' What is the answer and how does GraphQL handle this differently from REST versioning?
self_check_questions.answer: Answer: NO — adding a new field to an existing type is backwards compatible in GraphQL. Existing queries that don't request `deliveryFee` are completely unaffected (they just won't receive that field). Only clients that explicitly add `deliveryFee` to their query will receive it. In REST: adding a new JSON field to a response is also technically backwards compatible, but REST APIs often resort to versioning (/v1/, /v2/) when changing response shapes — because REST doesn't have a mechanism for clients to specify which fields they want, so you can't safely remove fields later without breaking clients. GraphQL: the field selection mechanism means you can add fields freely (safe) and deprecate old fields with @deprecated before removing them (giving clients time to migrate). REST equivalent would require versioning to safely add+remove fields. This is GraphQL's major operational advantage for APIs with multiple client teams.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Next: Spring for GraphQL — implement the schema in Spring Boot using @QueryMapping, @MutationMapping, and @SchemaMapping annotations.
